findings.description: SQL injection: the email parameter is interpolated into the login query via an f-string instead of a bound parameter, allowing authentication bypass and data exfiltration.
Inverted session-expiry check deletes and rejects still-valid sessions while returning the user_id for already-expired tokens, breaking auth correctness.
find_user_by_email runs 'SELECT * FROM users' and filters in Python, forcing a full-table scan and full-row materialization instead of a WHERE/index lookup.
Account lockout never expires: LOCKOUT_MINUTES is defined but unused and there is no timestamp, so a locked account stays locked permanently with no time-based reset.
User enumeration via timing/branch: unknown email returns immediately without password hashing while a valid email runs PBKDF2 (100k iterations), exposing whether an account exists.
datetime.utcnow() is deprecated (3.12+) and returns naive datetimes used for security-critical expiry math, risking timezone-confusion bugs.
Sessions live only in an in-memory dict, so all sessions are lost on restart and are not shared across worker processes, defeating horizontal scaling and persistence implied by db_path.
failed_attempts increment is committed but never bounded against concurrent logins, and the counter can grow unbounded past MAX_FAILED_ATTEMPTS without normalization.
Session tokens are stored as plaintext dict keys; a memory/heap disclosure or debug dump exposes live bearer tokens directly without hashing at rest.
